titleStmt.title: The Chairman of the American Delegation ( (Davis) ) to the Secretary of State

div.head: The Chairman of the American Delegation ( Davis ) to the Secretary of State

list.item: As a result of our telephone conversation I understand more clearly what was in your mind and appreciate your reasons for having the conversations ostensibly conducted through the Embassies until the Japanese arrive which will make it possible and expedient to institute the conversations in a more open way and eventually with a broader scope. My opinion is that what will determine the eventual success of the conversations with the Japanese will be the previous ironing out with the British of our differences on technical matters so that we can have a common viewpoint. It is, of course, difficult to tell just how much time will be required for this but perhaps not more than 2 weeks. It therefore seems to me that it would be advisable for me to proceed to London as planned and, after a talk with Leigh, get him started on technical discussions with Admiral Bellairs who, MacDonald told me, would represent the British. I could then absent myself on vacation somewhere near London where Leigh could get in touch with me at any time and more formally take up my job when the Japanese arrive or earlier if developments so warrant.

I will have to remain near London to keep in touch with developments on disarmament. The French have also expressed the hope that I will be in London when Barthou makes his visit in the early days of July.
Leaving tonight for Paris. Please reply there.
